title: TPU v1 · Cycle-by-Cycle Shifting Inside the Array vs. valid/ready Handshakes Between Blocks
oneLine: Adjacent PEs in the systolic array exchange data through regular local registers on one aligned clock, so no handshake is needed. valid/ready becomes necessary only across functional-block boundaries such as UB↔MXU↔accumulator, where rates or clocks may differ. Interface protocol depends on whether both sides share a deterministic cadence.

legend.name: Inter-PE activation register a
legend.desc: A staging register between adjacent PEs captures activation a one position to the right each cycle. It is a registered hard wire with no valid/ready sideband; arrival time is known from the cycle schedule.

legend.name: Inter-PE psum register
legend.desc: The partial sum moves down a column and is captured one position per cycle. Together with the rightward activation movement, it forms a two-dimensional synchronous flow.

legend.name: Synchronous cadence domain
legend.desc: The full array advances in lockstep on one clock, with no valid, ready, or backpressure. Regular synchrony yields an extremely simple interface and is a key source of systolic efficiency.

legend.name: valid path between blocks
legend.desc: Across a functional boundary such as UB→MXU, the producer sends data plus valid to state that the current-cycle payload is meaningful.

legend.name: ready backpressure between blocks
legend.desc: The consumer asserts ready when it can accept data. A transfer occurs only when valid and ready are both high in the same cycle. This negotiated complexity is unnecessary inside the array but useful between blocks.

steps.cap: ① Inside the array, activation a shifts right and partial sum psum shifts down through one staging register per hop. The interface contains only data and clock.

② The entire array advances in lockstep: every PE receives from its neighbor, computes once, and forwards to the next neighbor each cycle. No receiver acknowledgment is needed because arrival is scheduled.
③ At a block boundary such as UB→MXU, the producer sends data+valid. Rates or clocks may differ, so deterministic per-cycle arrival can no longer be assumed.
④ The consumer returns ready. Transfer occurs only when valid&ready are high together; deasserting ready applies backpressure and prevents upstream overflow.
⑤ The design rule: use handshake-free regular synchrony inside the array and negotiated valid/ready across rate-mismatched block boundaries. Choose based on whether both sides share the same cadence.

before: Classify two kinds of boundary: A tightly coupled, same-clock boundary with statically known cadence, such as PE↔PE, can use synchronous shifting without handshakes. A loosely coupled boundary whose rate or clock may differ, such as UB↔MXU↔accumulator or PCIe↔core, needs negotiation. Classify every connection before selecting a protocol.

Define the interface contract: For every cross-block path, specify data width, valid/ready use, backpressure support, byte/channel ordering, burst length, and reset values. This contract is expensive to change after blocks diverge.

Required inputs: Datapath width and supply cadence from architecture (③)—a 256-wide array consumes one activation row per cycle, approximately 256 bytes entering the matrix unit each cycle—plus block frequencies and clock-domain relationships.

Resolve the core assumption: Inside the array, static dataflow and lockstep PEs make arrival time calculable at design time. Once a boundary does not guarantee aligned cadence, that premise disappears and valid/ready must negotiate transfers at runtime.
during: Inside the array, replace handshakes with regular synchrony: PE↔PE uses one staging register; a moves right and psum moves down while one clock advances the whole array. With only data and clock, area, timing, and verification are minimal.

Between blocks, use valid/ready to decouple rates: At UB→MXU and MXU→accumulator boundaries, scheduling may not align every cycle. ready applies backpressure so a fast producer can wait for a slow consumer without losing or overflowing data.

Freeze handshake semantics: valid must not depend combinationally on ready; transfer occurs when valid&ready are high together; data remains stable while valid is high and ready is low. These rules prevent loops, drops, and duplicates when interfaces compose.

Decide how far backpressure travels: A running systolic wave should not stop mid-array because restarting would disturb alignment. Prefer buffers and scheduling before the array entrance to absorb rate differences instead of propagating backpressure through the PE grid.

Trade interface width against wiring: A cross-block port must support requirements such as 256 activations per cycle. A wider interface uses fewer handshakes but more routing; a narrower interface saves wires but needs more cycles or a higher clock. Balance against the boundary’s bandwidth budget.

Handle CDC honestly: TPU v1 does not publish its internal domain partition or CDC details. Standard datacenter-ASIC practice uses asynchronous FIFOs and multistage synchronizers at true clock crossings, such as PCIe↔core, while keeping the MAC array in one synchronous domain; this is not a disclosed v1 implementation detail.

result.label: In-array arrival time
result.value: Statically determined by cycle count at design time

result.label: Transfer condition
result.value: valid & ready high in the same cycle

result.label: Backpressure policy
result.value: Prefer buffering before the array; do not disrupt its cadence

result.label: Clock crossing (CDC)
result.value: Asynchronous FIFO + synchronizer at domain boundaries (common practice, not disclosed for v1)

after: Review every interface contract: Align width, valid/ready behavior, backpressure, and reset values between each producer and consumer. Mismatched interpretation of one wire is a common integration bug.

Check handshakes formally and in simulation: Assert that valid does not depend on ready, data stays stable under backpressure, and transfers are neither dropped nor duplicated. Inside the array, compare registered shifts against the cycle-level timing model.
Stress backpressure and boundaries: Cover sustained and intermittent ready=0, upstream bursts, the first transfer after reset, and handoff around array fill and drain.

Hand off to RTL (⑤): Implement synchronous shift registers, valid/ready logic, entrance buffers, and asynchronous FIFOs in synthesizable Verilog. Apply dedicated lint and timing constraints to CDC boundaries.

Update the integration view: Consolidate all cross-block interface contracts into a top-level connection table so integration and timing closure can detect wiring errors even when each block is locally correct.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (cycle-by-cycle local movement in the systolic array is described by the paper; cross-block valid/ready and CDC details follow common datacenter-inference ASIC practice rather than itemized v1 disclosure)
